topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon light filtering through the dusty window did little to lift the oppressive weight in my chest. Every sound in the shared apartment seemed amplified—the distant traffic, the hum of the refrigerator—all contributing to a dull, pervasive ache. I was hunched over my textbooks, trying to force concentration onto macroeconomics, but my focus kept fracturing. Then, I noticed it: my journal, usually tucked beneath my laptop, was sitting on the desk, open to a page I hadn't written yet. The handwriting was undeniably mine, yet the entries were filled with observations about my deepest insecurities, things I’d only ever let surface in the privacy of my own thoughts. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I slowly straightened up, my movements stiff, and peered over at Liam. He was sitting on the couch, ostensibly watching a documentary, but his eyes were fixed on the page. When he looked up, his expression was unreadable, a strange mixture of curiosity and something else—something that made the air around us feel suddenly thin.

[story 2]

It had been weeks since I’d felt anything resembling genuine spark. The world felt muted, like a photograph left too long in the sun. I spent most of my time staring blankly at my ceiling, the silence of the room pressing in on me like physical pressure. Today, I was wrestling with the urge to just curl up under my duvet and disappear. When I finally decided to move, I found my journal slightly askew. It wasn't just open; it was actively being read. I watched, frozen, as Liam traced a line of script with his finger, his brow furrowed in deep concentration. My breath hitched. A wave of intense vulnerability washed over me, quickly followed by a sharp, defensive tightening in my throat. I wanted to scream, but the sound caught somewhere behind my teeth. I couldn't look away, yet I desperately wanted to vanish into the floorboards.

[story 3]

The evening air in the apartment felt heavy, clinging to my skin like damp wool. I hadn't eaten much, and the effort of simply existing felt monumental. I was trying to write down some abstract thoughts in my notebook, trying to give shape to the swirling confusion inside, when I realized the notebook wasn't mine anymore. It was open on the coffee table, and Liam was leaning over it, his head tilted at an angle that made my skin crawl. His gaze was intense, absorbing every word as if it held some profound secret. A strange, hollow sensation settled in my core, a profound sense of exposure. My hands began to tremble slightly as I took a hesitant step forward. "Hey," I managed, my voice sounding thin and reedy. The sound seemed too loud in the quiet room, and Liam immediately looked up, a flicker of something—perhaps guilt, perhaps just acknowledgment—crossing his face before he quickly looked back down at the page.
